coredata.dc:title: Enhanced perovskite solar cells with TiO2-Graphene nanocomposite quantum dots in electron transport layer

coredata.dc:creator: Sucharitakul, Sukrit
Yarangsi, Vasan
Thanasanvorakun, Siripatsorn
Sintiam, Thanakrit
Yarin, Suparoek
Hongsith, Kritsada
Phadungdhitidhada, Surachet
Choopun, Supab
coredata.dc:description: Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs) have emerged as a promising platform for clean energy harvesting, thanks to their cost-effectiveness and low-temperature fabrication requirements. However, their widespread adoption is hindered by challenges such as charge trapping, and instability when exposed to UV light and humidity, preventing PSCs from becoming mainstream in solar cell production. PSCs consist of a perovskite (PSK) layer sandwiched between an electron transport layer (ETL) and a hole transport layer (HTL). Although TiO2 is widely used as the ETL due to its suitable band alignment, it suffers from low carrier mobility, poor stability under UV light, and thermal losses due to hot electron transfer. In this study, we enhance the ETL by incorporating Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) into a nanocomposite with TiO2 Quantum Dots, creating an interface between the mesoporous TiO2 and the PSK layer. This approach aims to improve charge transfer and reduce trapped states in PSCs via a one-step coating process. The TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots were synthesized via an electrochemical process and doped with titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP) at a concentration of 7.5 % w/w to create the ETL with the nanocomposite QDs interface. Our results demonstrate significant improvements in power conversion efficiency (PCE), fill factor (FF), short-circuit current density, and open-circuit voltage, along with a reduction in recombination rates, trap charge density, and charge transfer resistance. Notably, the PCE of devices with ETL modified by our nanocomposite QDs increased from 12.0 % in the control device to 15.1 %. The simplicity of our one-step coating process for TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots in colloidal form further supports the scalability and industrial viability of this method for PSC production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
Quantum dots
Electron transport layer
TiO2
Graphene

Trap state mitigation and charge transfer analysis
Concluding remarks on charge transfer improvement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematics of the electrochemical growth of TiO2 and GQDs nanocomposite with band alignment [22] as part of the ETL of the PSC.

SEM images of the perovskite (20kx with scale bar of 500 nm) demonstrating PSK surface of (a) control, (b) TiO2 QDs, (c) Ti-GQDs, (d) GQDs and (e) GQDs-Ti passivated TTIP ETL and (f) average perovskite grain size for each condition (for higher resolution SEM images of each layer c.f. our supplementary documents).

Plots of (a) current density vs applied potential difference plot of champion devices using different ETL treatment conditions in champion devices, (b) PCE, (c) FF, (d)
J
s c
and (e)
V
o c
statistical distribution.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of PSC done in (a) dark condition and (b) with 1-sun illumination.

(a) XRD spectra of Photoluminescence spectrum of PSK layer on differently passivated ETL and (b) intensity and FWHM extracted from (110) peak, (c) photoluminescence intensity of each condition and (d) TRPL results of each condition. Below are diagrams of TiO2 ETL e.) with TTIP as precursor for TiO2 layer (control), (f) with TiO2 QDs (or GQDs) as ion mediator into TiO2 and (g) with TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots infused into the TiO2 layer (Ti-GQDs condition).
Parameters obtain from J-V curve fitting for TFL.
Parameters obtained from EIS measurement in light and dark conditions.
Parameters obtained from double decay models on TRPL measurement extracted from Fig. 5d.

head.ce:title: Enhanced perovskite solar cells with TiO2-Graphene nanocomposite quantum dots in electron transport layer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cOffice of Research Administration, Chiang Mai University, Chiang Mai 50200, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs) have emerged as a promising platform for clean energy harvesting, thanks to their cost-effectiveness and low-temperature fabrication requirements. However, their widespread adoption is hindered by challenges such as charge trapping, and instability when exposed to UV light and humidity, preventing PSCs from becoming mainstream in solar cell production. PSCs consist of a perovskite (PSK) layer sandwiched between an electron transport layer (ETL) and a hole transport layer (HTL). Although TiO2 is widely used as the ETL due to its suitable band alignment, it suffers from low carrier mobility, poor stability under UV light, and thermal losses due to hot electron transfer. In this study, we enhance the ETL by incorporating Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) into a nanocomposite with TiO2 Quantum Dots, creating an interface between the mesoporous TiO2 and the PSK layer. This approach aims to improve charge transfer and reduce trapped states in PSCs via a one-step coating process. The TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots were synthesized via an electrochemical process and doped with titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP) at a concentration of 7.5 % w/w to create the ETL with the nanocomposite QDs interface. Our results demonstrate significant improvements in power conversion efficiency (PCE), fill factor (FF), short-circuit current density, and open-circuit voltage, along with a reduction in recombination rates, trap charge density, and charge transfer resistance. Notably, the PCE of devices with ETL modified by our nanocomposite QDs increased from 12.0 % in the control device to 15.1 %. The simplicity of our one-step coating process for TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots in colloidal form further supports the scalability and industrial viability of this method for PSC production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) were first developed in 2009 by Kojima [1]. Since the term was first coined, PSCs had extracted much of the interest from researchers as there have been over 16,000 citations within the past decades while performance of the PSCs have improved from 3.8 % [1] to over 25 % [2] through single conventional cell configuration. This makes the platform one of the highest potential candidates for clean energy sources. PSCs can be fabricated via spin coating in a relatively low temperature process in comparison to Si-based solar cells. This allows a relatively fast, cost-effective and scalable process for fabrication. However, PSCs suffer from instability from constant exposure to humidity and UV light limiting the performance over time of the platform. To state this problem, the issue lies within interlayer charge transfers and encapsulation of the PSCs. PSCs are composed of perovskite (PSK) layer sandwiched between electron transport layer (ETL) and hole transport layer (HTL) while the conductive glass and the conductor acting as the cell's electrodes are on the outer part. Regardless of how well the grains of PSK layer itself had been improved through the research and development process over the past decade through improved coating techniques and preparation for higher purity of perovskite precursor, the majority of the defects still reside on the PSK-HTL interface and PSK-ETL interface. This results in more trapped charges and defect states, limiting effective photoelectron transfer, short circuit current and, ultimately, the performance and the stability of the resulting solar cells.

To tackle such issues, implementation of mesoscopic scale ETL (for conventional configuration) and HTL (for inverted configuration) enhanced with nanomaterials such as quantum dots were employed. Studies have shown that the incorporation of nanomaterials can not only fill in the gap of the misaligned grain boundaries enhancing the interlayer carrier transfer. Improving not only the grain size but also open circuit voltage and the short circuit current. This ultimately resulted in improved Photoconversion Efficiency (PCE) and fill factor (FF). In conventional cells, usually Spiro-OMeTAD is used as HTL while the ETL is rotated among metal oxides such as TiO2, SnO2, ZnO. While SnO2 has relatively high carrier mobility, the band gap is small allowing more leakage and phase instability prohibits the stability of the ETL. ZnO has a wide band gap but the strong polarization in the z-axis of wurtzite structure, prohibiting higher carrier mobility. TiO2, while possessing appropriate band alignment and ideal band gap size for ETL, suffers from low carrier mobility and UV degradation. To tackle such issues, dopants and surface passivation have been introduced to TiO2 surface.

There have been considerable efforts in the scientific community that have demonstrated that surface passivation on TiO2 can successfully improve the material as a more effective ETL. Incorporation of TiO2 as QDs on mesoporous TiO2 ETL has also been shown to improve the short circuit current, the open circuit voltage, PCE, FF and also stability of PSCs with TiO2 as ETL. One of the options is the inclusion of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to the TiO2 layer. Graphene, consisting of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and possessing remarkable electronic, thermal, and mechanical characteristics, is widely employed in sensitized solar cells [3–5]. In TiO2/graphene composite structures, graphene can enhance electron transport within TiO2 films, reducing the likelihood of charge carrier recombination and consequently enhancing photoelectrical conversion efficiency [3–5]. Incorporating graphene frameworks into heterostructures with TiO2 has been shown to enhance charge transfer and its consistency, thereby facilitating electron transport [6]. By integrating two semiconductors, the probability of recombination of photo-generated charge carriers is minimized through local charge separation and increased diffusion length [7]. Due to quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs offer adjustable band gaps and exhibit excellent chemical stability [8,9]. Consequently, GQDs have sparked significant interest in various types of photovoltaic devices, including polymer solar cells, [10], silicon solar cells [11], dye-sensitized solar cells [12] and PSCs. Zhu et al. incorporated an ultra-thin layer of GQDs between the perovskite and ETL, observing a significant reduction in perovskite photoluminescence at 760 nm. This emphasized that GQDs could enable rapid electron tunneling in optoelectronic devices [13]. Furthermore, combining GQDs with TiO2 as ETL was also found to create optical quenching in photoluminescence reducing recombination rate by up to 75 % [13]. Ryu et al. employed O2 plasma treatment to integrate GQDs onto a blocking TiO2 layer, resulting in a uniform and compact film that could enhance the Jsc through rapid electron extraction and increase the device's recombination resistance [14]. TiO2 combined with GQDs on titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP) has also been shown to boost the short circuit current, thereby enhancing the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs [15], mesoscopic PSCs featuring a GQDs-doped TiO2 ETL have also been shown to exhibit superior performance, attributed to accelerated electron extraction. [16]. Furthermore, hot electron transport mechanism between GQDs and TiO2 heterostructures have been reported in fs timescale [17] while FRET essay has also been demonstrated in TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite as biosensors [18]. While GQDs have demonstrated efficacy as electron tunnels in optoelectronic devices, and similar trends have been observed for GQDs on mesoporous TiO2 ETL, the fabrication processes for these devices have traditionally been step-by-step. The simultaneous incorporation of GQDs and TiO2 QDs in colloidal form in a single step as an ETL offers promising advancements in coating recipes. However, this approach has not yet been reported for PSCs applications.

In this study, we introduce a novel approach that involves the one-step inclusion of heterostructured GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite QDs in colloidal form onto mesoporous TiO2 ETL prepared by TTIP. This streamlined process offers potential benefits for simplifying fabrication while enhancing the performance of PSCs, which were subsequently fabricated, tested, and characterized.

In this work, we employed the GQDs and TiO2 QDs prepared based on our previously proposed recipe [19–21] in solution-based form through electrochemical process. Ti and graphite rods were cleaned and prepared for electrochemical process in KCl 0.3 M mixed with citric acid 0.1 M in DI water solution. Applied potential difference between 2 symmetric electrodes in each step, E vs. Ti / V and E vs. graphite / V, with cells potential difference of 10–15 V DC was applied to the precursor rods (titanium and graphite, respectively) for 30 min to 1 hour just as previously reported [19]. The resulting colloidal solution was spin-coated on top of the ETL on the perovskite solar cells preparation process. For GQDs and TiO2 samples, only graphite and Ti rods were used respectively. For Ti-GQDs samples, Ti rods were used for growth at first prior to switching to graphite rods and vice versa for GQDs-Ti samples [19]. It is to be noted that electrode potentials for TiO2 QDs were measured using a symmetric titanium electrode configuration and are reported as E vs. Ti / V. For GQDs, a symmetric graphite electrode configuration was used, and potentials are reported as E vs. graphite / V. Since no standard reference electrode (e.g., Ag/AgCl or SCE) was employed, the reported potentials are relative to the respective electrode material.

Perovskite solar cells were prepared in conventional cell configuration. First, conductive glass FTO was cut in 2.5 × 2.0 cm2 size then ultrasonically cleaned in Alcanox, acetone, ethanol then DI water respectively prior to N2 blow dry followed by 15 mins of UV/Ozone treatment at room temperature. Afterward, ETL was prepared using commercial TTIP (Titanium (IV) isopropoxide, Sigma-Aldrich) dissolved in ethanol with 1:10 vol ratio. Afterward the TTIP solution was mixed with various QDs solution of concentration 7.5 % by volume prior to spin coating on FTO at 5000 rpm for 30 s prior to baking at 500 C for 1 hour in the ambient. The ETL layer was then treated with UV/ozone treatment for 15 min. Afterward, perovskite (PSK) layer precursor was prepared from 1 mmol of PbI2 (purity 99.999 %) mixed with 0.7 mmol of methanaminium iodine (MAI) and 0.3 mmol of formamidinium idodide (FAI) in 200 microlitre of Methylsulfinylmethane (DMSO) and 800 microlitre of N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent to create FA0.3MA0.7PBI3 precursor for PSK layer. The PSK layer was then spin coated on the ETL at 5000 rpm for 30 s with Chlorobenzene (CB) antisolvent being added dropwise 15 s into the spincoating. The resulting coating was baked at 100 C for 1 hour before further processing. The HTL precursor was then prepared with 50 mg commercially purchased Spiro-OMeTAD mixed in 5 mL of CB subjected to 24 h of continuous stirring. The HTL precursor solution was then added with 20.16 microlitre of tertbutyl pyridine mixed with bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)azanide dissolved in acetonitrile 12.25 microlitre prior to spin-coating at 4000 rpm for 30 s. A shadow mask with 2.5 × 2.0 cm2 opening was then used for silver evaporation of 100 nm forming the metal contact on the HTL side with 0.04 cm2 gaps. The schematics of the PSCs is as demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

This section presents a comprehensive analysis of the structural, electrical, and optoelectronic properties of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with electron transport layers (ETLs) modified using TiO2-Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) nanocomposites. Key findings include the enhanced grain size and crystallinity of the perovskite layer, improved photovoltaic performance, reduced trap states, and faster charge transfer dynamics. The results are discussed in detail through structural characterization, photovoltaic metrics, trap state analysis, and time-resolved photoluminescence, providing insights into the mechanisms underlying the observed improvements. These findings demonstrate the significant potential of TiO2-GQD nanocomposites for scalable and efficient PSC fabrication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure analysis of PSK microstructures with TiO2-graphene QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural properties of the perovskite (PSK) layer are critical for determining charge transport and overall device performance. This section investigates the microstructures formed on different ETLs, highlighting the influence of TiO2-Graphene QDs on grain size and crystallinity. Firstly, after the ETL was prepared, the PSK layer was deposited on top of the ETL. To study the effect of the ETL modification condition on the grain size, the ETL was prepared with pure TTIP and then TTIP doped with GQDs, TiO2 QDs, Ti-GQDs and GQDs-Ti respectively. The SEM results are as shown in Fig. 2(a) through 2(e). ImageJ software was used to determine the grain size of the PSK layer and the results are as shown in Fig. 2(f). The grain size of the perovskite layer is highest with the condition where TiO2/GQDs heterostructured QDs were used as ETL show the highest grain size averaged to 480 µm. This is likely due to the halide passivation on the GQDs obtained from the electrolyte through the electrochemical process. It had been well established that the K+ [23–26] and Cl− [23,27] ions between the PSK layer and the ETL, this could also imply that the different quantum dots’ structure could deliver the ions with different efficiency.

Device performance metrics such as power conversion efficiency (PCE), short-circuit current density (Jsc), and fill factor (FF) provide insight into the effectiveness of ETL modifications. This section evaluates the photovoltaic performance of devices fabricated with TiO2-Graphene QDs compared to control samples. Thus, the fabricated devices were electrically tested under the sunlight simulator yielding the J-V curves as demonstrated in Fig. 3 . PSCs characteristics such as, PCE, FF, VOC and sJSC , were obtained through conventional fittings with diode models as demonstrated in Fig. 3.

As can be seen from Fig. 3(a) through 3 (e), the Ti-GQDs and GQDs-Ti samples showed the highest Jsc while Ti-GQDs showed the highest FF and VOC . The high
J
s c
is likely attributed to the faster charge transfer from TiO2-GQDs interface [13]. Although small amount of GQDs in the films had little influence on the total absorbance, which is dominated by the much more strongly absorbing and thicker perovskite layer, the effective delivery of the potassium and halide ions through different structures of QDs might have been mostly attributed for the improved crystal grain size and increased current density. While the reported PCE of ∼15 % may seem modest compared to recent advancements exceeding 20 %, this work highlights the novelty of employing a one-step coating process for TiO2-GQDs nanocomposites. This innovative approach ensures scalability and cost-efficiency while addressing key challenges in device fabrication, including stability and reproducibility. However, it is important to note that the experimental setup, including the use of a homemade glovebox for device fabrication, introduces certain limitations. Additionally, to ensure consistency and accurate comparison of our results, all devices—including both control and modified samples—were fabricated under the same conditions in this glovebox. Despite these constraints, the primary focus of our study is the development of a novel one-step coating process for TiO2-GQD nanocomposites, which offers significant advantages in terms of scalability, cost-efficiency, and improved stability. These aspects have been emphasized in the revised manuscript, along with a detailed explanation of the factors contributing to the observed PCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trap state mitigation and charge transfer analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Trap states at the PSK/ETL interface can severely impact charge transfer and device efficiency. Here, we analyze trap density, charge transfer, and recombination resistance using trap-filled-limit voltage (V
TFL ) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The trap density can be extracted from the plot of log-log scale of the J-V curve via the relation of trap-filled-limit voltage given in Eq. (1)
(1)
V
T F L
=
e
n t
L
2
2 ϵ
ϵ 0
e.g. the limit where low-voltage regime transitions to the trap filling regime (data provided in our supplementary materials in figure S1(a) through S1(e)). The investigation into trap-filled limit voltage estimation within PSCs underscores the significance of halide and potassium ion delivery via quantum dots. In the context of perovskite solar cells, where trap states profoundly influence device efficiency, the utilization of quantum dots as carriers of halide ions (Cl−) and alkaline ions (K+) offers a promising avenue for mitigating trap-filled limit voltage and reducing trap charge density. Through the delivery ionic passivation, defect sites within the perovskite lattice are effectively passivated, suppressing trap-mediated recombination pathways and enhancing device performance.

The PSCs fabricated with the heterostructured QDs demonstrated higher conductivity likely due to improved patching between the grain boundaries of PSK/ETL layer, as hot electrons are transferred from PSK layer to the ETL can be more efficiently absorbed and transferred to the contact layer with the GQDs heterostructures. To further investigate in this light, different conductivity regimes are explored through the contact I-V curve as demonstrated in Fig. 3.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to provide detailed insights into the electron charge transfer mechanisms at the interfaces, focusing on the phenomena of charge transport and recombination resistance in PSCs with different electron transport layers (ETLs). Both dark and light illuminated EIS measurements were conducted to evaluate the recombination resistance (
R
r e c
) in the dark mode and charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) in the light mode, using the equivalent circuit models as shown in Fig. 4 and extracted in Table 1 , similar to previously reported work on SnO with GQDs passivation [28]. Fig. 4 presents the Nyquist plots for PSCs with various ETLs, including pure TiO2, GQDs, TiO2 QDs, GQDs-TiO2 (noted as GQDs-Ti recipe), and TiO2-GQDs (noted as Ti-GQDs recipe) nanocomposites collected in dark condition (Fig. 4(a)) and 1-sun illumination condition (Fig. 4(b)) whose fitting parameters are represented in Table 2 . Here,
R s
represents the series resistance,
R
c t
denotes the charge transfer resistance, and
R
r e c
indicates the recombination resistance. Among the different configurations, the TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite ETL yielded the best results, demonstrating significantly improved charge transfer and reduced recombination resistance, leading to enhanced overall device performance. Specifically, the TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite exhibited the lowest series resistance (
R s
) of 12.08 Ω, the lowest charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) of 589.44 Ω, and the highest recombination resistance (
R
r e c
) of 18,369 Ω. These results indicate superior electron transport and reduced charge recombination, which contribute to the enhanced efficiency and stability of the PSCs. On the other hand, it is also note-worthy that TiO2 QD-based devices showed slightly higher
R
c t
(909.51 Ω) and lower
R
r e c
(10,695 Ω) compared to TiO2-GQDs devices, which explains their relatively lower PCE as shown in Fig. 3. These results highlight the importance of using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to enhance the charge transport properties and suppress recombination in TiO2-based ETLs, demonstrating the potential of TiO2-GQDs nanocomposites for high-performance PSCs.

Furthermore, the fabricated devices were further analyzed by XRD technique to determine the crystallinity of the samples. To obtain data provided in Fig. 5(a) through 5(d), the samples were prepared up to the PSK layer and were subjected to XRD analysis. The results in Fig. 5(a) showed peaks belonging to (110), (211), (220), (310) and (220) of PSK [29–31] with some of the FTO substrate peaks. Intensity and full-width half maxima (FWHM) of the (110) peaks on each condition were plotted together in comparison as shown in Fig. 5(b) [32]. This demonstrated that the samples with heterostructured QDs as ETL had the best intensity and lowest FWHM implying highest crystallinity of the PSK through passivation of ETL favorable for coating of the PSK layer [32]. This is also in good agreement with photoluminescence results provided in Fig. 5(c) where at a control illumination, Ti-GQDs ETL condition showed the least photoluminescence intensity at the steady state PL implying the least recombination in the coating condition.

Understanding the time-dependent behavior of charge transfer is crucial for optimizing interfacial dynamics. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements reveal the decay characteristics and provide insights into charge extraction efficiency for various ETLs. For the TRPL analysis, after the device was illuminated with 532 nm pulse laser, the time-binning detection at 760 nm was probed for perovskite peaks. The probed intensity was then fitted with the double exponential decay models given in Eq. (2):
(2)
I
( t )
=
A 1
e
− t /
T 1
+
A 2
e
− t /
T 2
+ B
Where
T 1
and
T 2
are fast and slow decay mechanism while
A 1
and
A 2
are the intensity of the corresponding mechanism respectively and B is the background constant. The time constants were extracted from Fig. 5(d). as reported in Table 3 accordingly.

From Table 3, the Ti-GQDs condition yielded the fastest average effective charge transfer. In incorporation with the EIS results, the Ti-GQDs ETL is a more prominent candidate for faster electron transport due to less charge transfer resistance and larger grain boundaries allowing more efficient electron transfer in both lateral and vertical direction (also thanks to graphene nanocomposite yielding higher electron mobility) [13]. As illustrated in Fig. 5(e) through Fig. 5(g), in the case of TiO2 ETL without any passivation, the defects can easily form between ETL and PSK (Fig. 5(e)) while incorporating TiO2 QDs or GQDs the QDs can mediate the defects through ionic passivation on the surface (Fig. 5(f)) yielding improved grain boundaries after the coating of PSK. While TiO2 QDs may have low carrier mobility and directly incorporating GQDs into the TiO2 ETL may not yield good material composition, incorporating TiO2-GQDs nano composite quantum dots solves both issues allowing Ti-GQDs to become the ETL doping recipe that yielded the transport properties most suitable for electron extraction from PSK layer as illustrated in Fig. 5(g). All these results are in good agreement with the PSCs improved performance, grain size, and crystallinity via the combined nanocomposite while reducing trapped charge between ETL/PSK interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concluding remarks on charge transfer improvement
ce:section.ce:para:
Overall, the improvement in PCE is chiefly due to the improved FF of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with TiO2-GQDs nanocomposites. This can be attributed to several interrelated factors, primarily involving enhanced charge transport properties, reduced
R s
and lower recombination losses through increased
R
r e c
. As shown in Table 2, the TiO2-GQDs-based devices exhibit the highest FF of 79.2 %, compared to 75.1 % in the control devices, indicating a more efficient charge extraction process at the electron transport layer (ETL) interface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results (Fig. 4) reveal that the
R s
in Ti-GQDs devices is significantly lower at 12.08 Ω, compared to 24.05 Ω in the control devices. This reduction suggests that the TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite forms a more conductive ETL, facilitating faster electron transport and minimizing ohmic losses. Additionally, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) quenching measurements indicate enhanced charge extraction efficiency at the perovskite/ETL interface. The relative quenching efficiency, calculated from steady-state PL intensity (from Fig. 5(c)) as
(3)
Q u e n c h i n g
E f f i c i e n c y =
I
c o n t r o l
−
I
T i − G Q D s
I
c o n t r o l
x 100 %
shows a reduction of over 30 % in steady-state PL intensity for Ti-GQDs PSCs devices, indicating more effective charge separation and reduced recombination at the interface, which contributes to the higher FF. Furthermore, EIS analysis under 1-sun illumination conditions demonstrates that Ti-GQDs devices exhibit a lower charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) of 589.44 Ω compared to 837.82 Ω in the control devices, while the recombination resistance (
R
r e c
) increased from 12,040 Ω in the control devices to 18,369 Ω in TiO2-GQDs devices. These changes reflect a reduction in non-radiative recombination and improved charge transport across the ETL, further supporting the observed FF enhancement. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) results in Fig. 5(a) reveal a narrower full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the (110) peak for TiO2-GQDs-treated samples, indicating improved crystallinity of the perovskite layer. Higher crystallinity correlates with fewer grain boundaries and defects, leading to better charge mobility and reduced recombination losses. In addition, the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the TiO2 matrix enhances ionic passivation at the perovskite/ETL interface by facilitating the delivery of halide ions (Cl⁻) and potassium ions (K⁺) to defect sites, as observed from
V
T F L
analysis. This ionic passivation reduces trap density and improves the charge transport environment. The combined effects of reduced series resistance, enhanced charge extraction efficiency, lower recombination losses, improved crystallinity, and ionic passivation contribute to the significant increase in FF observed in TiO2-GQDs devices. These findings highlight the potential of TiO2-GQDs nanocomposites for scalable and efficient photovoltaic applications.

In conclusion, this study presents advancement in addressing the persistent challenges encountered by PSCs through the utilization of TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite to enhance charge transfer and mitigate trap states. Our research focused on optimizing the interface between the PSK layer and the ETL, typically composed of TiO2, by incorporating heterostructured TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite quantum dots in nanocomposite forms. Synthesized via an electrochemical process and doped with TTIP, these nanocomposited QDss facilitated improved grain size, reduced trap charge density, and reduced the chiefly unfavorable recombination as shown by our TRPL, EIS, XRD and J-V measurements. Our reported Ti-GQDs recipe not only brought out uniform integration of TiO2 QDs but also the nanocomposited GQDs in the recipe enhanced the charge transfer properties making the ETL/PSK interface more favorable for electrons extraction in PSCs. Devices incorporating TiO2-GQDs nanocomposite exhibited higher PCE,
J s c
,
V
O C
and FF compared to control samples, underscoring the efficacy of this approach. The simplicity and efficacy of this one-step nanocomposite QDs coating process highlight its potential for scalability and widespread application, positioning QDs nanocomposite as a promising avenue for advancing the efficiency and stability of PSCs for renewable energy deployment. Furthermore, the coating of these nanocomposited quantum dots was achieved in a one-step process, utilizing a colloidal solution, thereby offering a straightforward and potentially commercially viable approach.

Sukrit Sucharitakul: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Vasan Yarangsi: Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Siripatsorn Thanasanvorakun: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Thanakrit Sintiam: Formal analysis, Data curation. Suparoek Yarin: Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kritsada Hongsith: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Surachet Phadungdhitidhada: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Supab Choopun: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work (Grant No RGNS 63 – 079) was supported by Office of the Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation (OPS MHESI), Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2025.145901.
